retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrointestinal Hormonal Control -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hormones undergo synthesis in specialized cells of the GI tract mucosa known as enteroendocrine cells. Enteroendocrine cells are specialized endoderm-derived epithelial cells that originate from stem cells located at the base of intestinal crypts. These cells are dispersed throughout the GI mucosa, sprinkled in between epithelial cells from the stomach all the way through to the colon. Also, these enteroendocrine cells possess hormone-containing granules concentrated at the basolateral membrane, adjacent to capillaries, that secrete their hormones via exocytosis in response to a wide range of stimuli related to food intake. These stimuli include small peptides, amino acids, fatty acids, oral glucose, distension of an organ, and vagal stimulation. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nutrient Absorption -- Organ Systems Involved -- Endocrine
retrieved_docs.content: Organs of the gastrointestinal system have vital endocrine functions. For example, the pancreas secretes insulin (beta cells) and glucagon (alpha cells), which are crucial in blood glucose regulation and energy expenditure. [24] The intestines absorb cholesterol, which is important in steroid hormone biosynthesis. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nutrient Absorption -- Cellular Level -- The Cells of the Gastrointestinal Tract
retrieved_docs.content: Enteroendocrine cells : These cells are located in the stomach, pancreas, and small intestine. Enteroendocrine cells have several roles. First, they are responsible for biogenic amine and polypeptide secretion, releasing ghrelin, cholecystokinin, glucagon-like peptide 1, peptide YY, insulin-like peptide 5, and oxyntomodulin. Second, they aid in food digestion and nutrient absorption. [7] Third, they aid in pathogen recognition by expressing toll-like receptors and commensal bacterial metabolite-sensitive receptors. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrointestinal Hormonal Control -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: GIP is secreted by K cells in the duodenum and jejunum in response to glucose, amino acids, and fatty acids. GIP is the only GI hormone with a response to all three macronutrient types, and newer studies suggest that changes in intraluminal osmolarity may be what stimulates GIP secretion. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrointestinal Hormonal Control -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The enteroendocrine cells responsible for synthesizing and secreting GI hormones derive from pluripotent intestinal stem cells in the intestinal crypts. As these stem cells move up the crypt-villus axis, they express specific transcription factors that give rise to absorptive enterocytes or cells of secretory lineages (Paneth cells, goblet cells, and enteroendocrine cells). The sequential expression of three basic loop helix loop (bHLH) transcription factors (Math1, Neurogenin3, and NeuroD1) is involved in specifying the enteroendocrine cell lineage. Math1 expression specifies cells that are fated for the secretory progenitor lineage and segregates them from the absorptive enterocyte lineage. Subsequent expression of Neurogenin3 represents a secretory progenitor cell that has initiated differentiation into the endocrine cell lineage. Lastly, NeuroD1 expression induces cell cycle arrest and commits a cell to an enteroendocrine fate. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Hormones -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The endocrine hormones are a wide array of molecules that traverse the bloodstream to act on distant tissues, leading to alterations in metabolic functions within the body. They can broadly divide into peptides, steroids, and tyrosine derivatives that may work on either cell surface or intracellular receptors. A discussion on the synthesis, structures, and mechanisms of action of these hormones will follow.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Islets of Langerhans -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Each pancreatic endocrine cell releases 1 specific hormone. The alpha cells release glucagon, a peptide hormone that raises the concentration of glucose and fatty acids in the bloodstream and is the primary catabolic hormone in the body. The beta cells release insulin, a peptide hormone that decreases glucose concentration in the blood by stimulating glucose uptake by the liver, skeletal muscle, and adipose tissue via specialized receptors. The delta cells release somatostatin, which inhibits glucagon and insulin secretion. The epsilon cells release ghrelin, a hormone that stimulates appetite, increases fat storage, and stimulates the growth hormone release from the pituitary gland. The PP cells release pancreatic polypeptide, a satiety hormone that decreases gastric acid secretion, emptying, and upper intestinal motility. These cells communicate through extracellular spaces and gap junctions and influence each other's release of hormones. For example, glucagon release by alpha cells suppresses insulin release by beta cells and vice versa, and somatostatin release by delta cells inhibits glucagon and insulin secretion by the alpha and beta cells. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrointestinal Hormonal Control -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The GI hormones classify as endocrines, paracrine, or neurocrine based on the method by which the molecule gets delivered to its target cell(s). Endocrine hormones are secreted from enteroendocrine cells directly into the bloodstream, passing from the portal circulation to the systemic circulation, before being delivered to target cells with receptor-specificity for the hormone. The five GI hormones that qualify as endocrines are gastrin, cholecystokinin (CCK), secretin, glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP), and motilin. Enteroendocrine cells also secrete paracrine hormones, but they diffuse through the extracellular space to act locally on target tissues and do not enter the systemic circulation. Two examples of paracrine hormones are somatostatin and histamine. Additionally, some hormones may operate via a combination of endocrine and paracrine mechanisms. These “candidate” hormones are glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1), pancreatic polypeptide, and peptide YY. Lastly, neurocrine hormones get secreted by postganglionic non-cholinergic neurons of the enteric nervous system. Three neurocrine hormones with significant physiologic functions in the gut are vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP), gastrin release peptide (GRP), and enkephalins. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrointestinal Hormonal Control -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The primary function of the gastrointestinal tract is to supply nutrients to our bodies via the processes of ingestion, motility, secretion, digestion, and absorption; this occurs through complex coordination of digestive processes that are regulated by intrinsic endocrine and nervous systems. Although the nervous system exerts influence on many digestive processes, the GI tract is the largest endocrine organ in the human body and produces numerous mediators that play an integral role in regulating functions of the GI tract.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ability of cells to communicate is crucial for maintaining cell function and homeostasis. Cells communicate through gap junctions, juxtacrine signaling, or secreted chemical messengers. Gap junctions enable the direct transfer of signaling molecules between the cytoplasm of 2 connected cells. Juxtacrine signaling involves a membrane-bound chemical messenger that directly interacts with a receptor on the plasma membrane of an adjacent cell. Secreted chemical messengers are released from the originating cell and are classified into the following 4 types: Paracrine signaling molecules: target neighboring cells close to the release site. Autocrine signaling molecules: target the same cell that secreted them. Hormones: are released by endocrine cells and reach target cells in one or more distant body locations via blood circulation. Neurotransmitters: are released by neurons and affect other neurons or effector cells near the release site.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrointestinal Hormonal Control -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: GLP-1 is also produced in the small intestine and secreted from L cells. The presence of hexose and fat stimulate its release. Pancreatic polypeptide and peptide YY are secreted by protein and fat, respectively, although their functions are still relatively unknown.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Messengers -- Organ Systems Involved
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple organ systems are involved in cellular communication. Some of these organ systems are listed here [13] [14] : Endocrine organs: Examples of endocrine organs include the thyroid gland, adrenal glands, pancreas, and pituitary gland; these organs use a hematogenous spread of hormones to transmit signals to target cells far away. Nervous system: The synapsis between neurons or in the neuromuscular junction is a type of paracrine signaling. A neuron releases a neurotransmitter in the synaptic cleft, and nearby cells (other neurons or muscle cells) receive signals through receptors to neurotransmitters.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrointestinal Hormonal Control -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The release of GI hormones is in response to input from G-protein-coupled receptors that detect changes in luminal contents. Some of these receptors only respond to selective luminal substances and subsequently release GI hormones from their respective enteroendocrine cells through unknown mechanisms. Overall, gastrointestinal hormones manage a diverse set of actions in the body including: Contraction and relaxation of smooth muscle wall and sphincters Secretion of enzymes for digestion Secretion of fluid and electrolytes Trophic (growth) effects on tissues of GI tract Regulating secretion of other GI peptides (i.e., somatostatin inhibits secretion of all GI hormones)

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hormones of the endocrine system are a vast topic with numerous hormones involved, affecting virtually every organ in the human body. Human physiologic processes such as homeostasis, metabolic demand, development, and reproduction are all possible because of hormones and the processes mediated by their actions. This review elaborates on the organs that secret the specific hormone, the actions of the hormone, and where these actions occur. Also, it reviews several of the most common endocrine diseases involving hormones. The number of diseases covered is not comprehensive due to the extensive number of diseases and ongoing research in this area. Understanding the physiology of hormones and how they result in pathological conditions is important.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Pancreas
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin has many influences on other hormones within the body. The pancreatic islet cells have alpha, beta, and delta cells. Alpha secretes glucagon, beta secret insulin, and delta secretes somatostatin. When these hormones get secreted, they have paracrine effects on the surrounding cells. Insulin specifically reaches alpha cells first and inhibits the release of glucagon, which causes an increased effect of its metabolic actions. In hyperglycemic states, somatostatin can also be secreted, inhibiting alpha cells from releasing glucagon to reduce glucose levels. [87] [88] [89] [90] [91]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nutrient Absorption -- Cellular Level -- The Cells of the Gastrointestinal Tract
retrieved_docs.content: G-cells : These are neuroendocrine cells in the stomach antrum and the duodenum. They are responsible for secreting the hormone gastrin, which stimulates enterochromaffin-like cells and parietal cells. Enterochromaffin-like cells secrete histamine, while parietal cells release hydrochloric acid in the stomach. Gastrin also promotes gastric mucosal growth and supports gastric motility. G-cells are innervated by the vagus nerve and stimulated by gastrin-releasing peptide and bombesin. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

Together, these relationships illustrate how diverse pharmaceutical formulations can combine multiple hormone‑based actives, how ACTH fits within the endocrine hierarchy linking pituitary hormones to uterotonic activity, and how peptide hormones such as ACTH, erythropoietin, and glucagon are unified by their proteinaceous nature while serving different regulatory functions in the body. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in recognizing the multifaceted actions of hormone‑containing products and their potential systemic effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778
retrieved_docs.content: that can disrupt cellular membranes and provoke immune reactions, while human insulin is the principal hormone governing carbohydrate metabolism and energy storage. Toxoids, which are inactivated toxins, are used to safely stimulate protective immune physiology without causing disease. Together, these diverse organisms, molecules, anatomical structures, and pharmacologic agents illustrate the wide range of factors that influence human physiological processes and underscore their importance in clinical assessment, diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1347
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is categorized under the disposition of hormones, indicating its role as a hormonal agent. It is a member of the group termed “parathyroid hormones and analogues,” linking it to other related endocrine substances. One of the key physiologic effects of parathormon is the stimulation of fluid and electrolyte absorption in the small intestine, highlighting its importance in maintaining intestinal electrolyte balance and overall mineral homeostasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon, also known as adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), is classified as a member of the peptidy group, meaning it is a peptide hormone that functions within the body’s endocrine signaling pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: as their anatomical target, underscoring the system’s role in hormonal regulation beyond the thyroid alone. Overall, the knowledge base links specific thyroid‑acting drugs, their classification, and their physiological targets with diagnostic and therapeutic procedures that focus on the endocrine system, while highlighting the hierarchical and functional relationships among thyroid preparations, thyroid hormones, the thyroid gland, pancreatic hormones, and broader endocrine functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2955
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists constitute a class of compounds that stimulate the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone receptor to modulate pituitary hormone release, and triptorelin is a specific agent that belongs to this LHRH‑agonist class. Recognizing triptorelin as a member of the LHRH‑agonist group is essential for understanding its mechanism of action and therapeutic applications in hormone‑dependent conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenolytic hormones are a class of hormones that stimulate the breakdown of glycogen into glucose, thereby increasing blood glucose levels. Pancreatic hormones, which are secreted by the pancreas, fall within this category because they include glucagon, a key glycogenolytic hormone that promotes glycogenolysis. This relationship highlights the role of pancreatic endocrine function in regulating glucose metabolism through glycogenolytic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, and they are considered members of the progesterone family. As such, they function as hormonal agents that influence reproductive physiology and genital system regulation, sharing the core structural and functional characteristics of progesterone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 440

Recognizing this shared classification is important for understanding their mechanisms of action, pharmacokinetics, and therapeutic applications, particularly when ACTH is incorporated into complex multi‑ingredient formulations such as the oral liquid product described above.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1601
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol is classified as a hormone, reflecting its role as a key endogenous steroid that regulates reproductive physiology. Gonadotropiny, representing the family of gonadotropins, is a subclass within the broader hormone category, indicating that gonadotropins share fundamental hormonal properties. The group termed “Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain” includes estradiol as one of its members, situating estradiol among both naturally occurring and synthetically derived estrogenic compounds used in clinical practice. Choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are both identified as having the structural class gonadotropiny, meaning they belong to the gonadotropin family and share similar molecular architecture and biological functions related to stimulation of the gonads. Together, these relationships delineate how estradiol fits within the hormone hierarchy, how gonadotropins are organized as a hormonal subclass, and how specific agents such as choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are structurally related to the gonadotropin class, informing both pharmacologic classification and therapeutic application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1839
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist and Progestogens, endocrine therapy drugs are both classified as hormones and related agents. Within the category of Progestogens, endocrine therapy drugs, medroxyprogesteron is identified as a specific member, illustrating that medroxyprogesteron is a particular progestogenic agent used in endocrine therapy. This organization underscores that Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist and the broader class of progestogenic endocrine therapy drugs share a common hormonal classification, with medroxyprogesteron representing a concrete example of a progestogen employed in therapeutic regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 551
retrieved_docs.content: to the broader class of hypothalamic and pituitary hormones. Within hierarchical classifications, hypothalamic and pituitary hormones are considered a subclass of uterotonics—agents that stimulate uterine contraction. Uterotonics themselves are linked to vasotocin, a peptide hormone closely related to oxytocin that also exerts uterine‑contractile effects. From a biochemical perspective, ACTH, erythropoietin, and glucagon are all members of the “amino acid, peptide, or protein” family, reflecting their nature as peptide hormones derived from amino‑acid chains. This inverse‑ISA relationship underscores that these hormones share fundamental structural characteristics despite their distinct physiological roles: ACTH stimulates adrenal cortisol production, erythropoietin regulates red‑blood‑cell formation, and glucagon raises blood glucose levels. Together, these relationships illustrate how diverse pharmaceutical formulations can combine multiple hormone‑based actives, how ACTH fits

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Mifepristone is classified within the group of progesterone receptor modulators, sex hormones, and modulators of the genital system, indicating that it acts on hormonal pathways related to reproductive function. In addition, mifepristone functions as a hormone antagonist, meaning it blocks the action of certain endogenous hormones, particularly progesterone, thereby influencing processes that depend on progesterone signaling. This dual characterization highlights mifepristone’s role both as a member of a specific pharmacologic class and as an antagonist of hormonal activity, which is central to its therapeutic applications in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2033
retrieved_docs.content: Hormones and their metabolites and precursors are classified as a subclass within the broader category of steroid hormones, indicating that steroid hormones serve as the parent classification for these compounds. This hierarchical relationship underscores that steroid hormones encompass a range of hormone molecules, their metabolic derivatives, and precursor substances, which is important for understanding hormone biosynthesis, metabolism, and related pharmacological interventions.
